data.title: Duke Energy's Florida unit to drop storm cost recovery charge from customer bills a month early
data.description:
Duke Energy (DUK) said Wednesday its Florida unit will remove the storm cost recovery charge related to the company's ~$1.1B response to hurricanes Debby, Helene and Milton from customer bills a month earlier than originally scheduled.

Duke (DUK) said residential customers can expect a ~$33 reduction on their monthly bills starting in February for every 1,000 kWh of electricity they use, and another ~$11 cut in March, bringing total reductions on customer bill to ~$44 from January levels.

Commercial and industrial customers also will see their monthly bills lowered between 9.6% and 15.8% from January levels, although the specific impact will vary based on usage and other factors.

Duke Energy Florida (DUK) owns 12,300 MW of energy capacity, supplying electricity to 2M residential, commercial and industrial customers across a 13,000 sq mile service area in the state.

data.title: Soho House jumps after securing $200M in alternative commitments to fund merger
data.description:
Soho House (SHCO) soared 13% in post-market trading after disclosing about $200 million in alternative commitments to fund the close of its planned merger.

The commitments include a $50 million equity letter from Morse Ventures, which is owned by the chairman/CEO of MCR Investors, according to an 8-K filing on Wednesday. MCR also notified Soho it will be committing $50 million in equity under the Original MCR Commitment for a total of $100 million in equity commitments from Morse Ventures and MCR. 

The alternative funding comes after Soho House (SHCO) shares slid 10% last Thursday after the company said MCR Hospitality Fund won't be able to fully fund  its $200 million commitment for the company’s pending sale.

Soho House (SHCO) in August agreed to be acquired for $9 a share by an investor group led by MCR Hotels.

data.title: Boeing flagged prior engine-mount failures before fatal UPS crash
data.description:
Boeing (BA) had flagged past failures of a structural component later found cracked on the cargo jet involved in November’s fatal United Parcel Service (UPS) crash, according to a mid-investigation update from U.S. safety officials.

Investigators with the National Transportation Safety Board on Wednesday said Boeing (BA) issued a service notice in early 2011. The notification alerted MD-11 operators to multiple prior failures of a spherical bearing race, a part designed to absorb stress and vibration in the engine mounting system. Boeing (BA) indicated at the time that inspections of the component would be added to routine maintenance checks.

The agency is now examining how those inspection recommendations were reflected in Boeing’s (BA) maintenance manuals and how UPS (UPS) applied them, along with any related communications between Boeing (BA) and the Federal Aviation Administration. Boeing (BA) previously concluded the issue didn’t pose an immediate flight safety risk.

Following the crash, the FAA temporarily grounded MD-11F cargo aircraft while operators conducted inspections and corrective actions, later extending those requirements to planes with similar engine-pylon designs.

A preliminary investigation found fatigue cracking in several engine-mount structures on the McDonnell Douglas MD-11F, which lost its left engine shortly after takeoff from Louisville. The aircraft failed to gain altitude and crashed moments later, killing the crew and people on the ground.

The MD-11, no longer produced, is now flown exclusively in cargo service.

data.title: Chevron expected to secure expanded Venezuela license from U.S. this week - Reuters
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) is expected to receive an expanded Venezuela license from the U.S. government this week that could allow for increased production and exports, Reuters reported Wednesday.

Chevron (CVX) likely will be one of several U.S. companies to secure approvals from the Trump administration to conduct business in Venezuela as oil companies, according to the report, which also said Marathon Petroleum (MPC) is in talks with U.S. officials to receive Venezuelan crude for its refineries, and that Valero Energy (VLO) and global traders Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) and Mercuria also have been in discussions for licenses to do business with Venezuela.

The U.S. has completed the first sales of Venezuelan oil that are part of a $2B deal between the two countries, and additional sales are expected in the coming days and weeks, Reuters also reported.

Chevron (CVX) and global trading houses Vitol and Trafigura reportedly have been competing for U.S. government deals to export the Venezuelan crude.

Chevron (CVX) is the only U.S. oil major currently producing crude in Venezuela, with ~240K bbl/day in joint ventures with state-run PDVSA; Chevron shares have gained nearly 9% since U.S. forces removed Venezuela's Maduro from power earlier this month.

data.title: Trump issues proclamation imposing 25% tariff on Nvidia H200, AMD MI325X chips
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump issued a proclamation on Wednesday imposing a 25% tariff on certain high-end chips, including Nvidia's (NVDA) H200 and AMD's (AMD) MI325X.

"This tariff will not apply to chips that are imported to support the buildout of the U.S. technology supply chain and the strengthening of domestic manufacturing capacity for derivatives of semiconductors," the White House said in a statement. "In the near future, President Trump may impose broader tariffs on imports of semiconductors and their derivative products, as well as an accompanying tariff offset program to incentivize domestic manufacturing as previously announced."

The proclamation was related to Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962. The U.S. Secretary of Commerce also completed an investigation under Section 232 regarding the importance of domestic production of semiconductors and semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

The investigation determined "that present quantities and circumstances of the imports of semiconductors, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and their derivative products threaten to impair national security." 

"Today's action will address the threat to national security by, among other things, incentivizing domestic production of semiconductors and reducing our nation's reliance on foreign sources and foreign supply chains," the White House said.

On Tuesday, the Trump Administration formally approved sales of Nvidia's H200 chips to China, establishing a new regulatory framework that could pave the way for shipments of the H200 despite strong opposition from China hawks in Washington.

data.title: Core Silver grants stock options
data.description:
• Core Silver (CCOOF)  announced on Wednesday that it has granted stock options to acquire up to 1.75M common shares.
  • The options, exercisable at $0.81 for four years, and the underlying shares are subject to a four-month-and-one-day hold period.
  • The company also granted 1.35M restricted share units, each representing one common share, which will vest 12 months from the date of grant.

data.title: Millennium Silver announces $2.25M private placement and stock option issuance
data.description:
• Millennium Silver (MSC:CA) on Wednesday stated that it plans a non-brokered private placement of up to 150M units at $0.015 per unit to raise gross proceeds of up to $2.25M.
  • Each unit will comprise one common share and one non-transferable warrant exercisable for one additional common share at $0.05 per share during the first three years and $0.10 per share in years four and five.
  • Closing of the offering is subject to customary closing conditions.
  • The company also granted 8.16M stock options exercisable at $0.05 per share for up to 8.16M common shares, expiring January 14, 2031.

data.title: Royal Caribbean placed on Citi's downside 30-day watch ahead of Q4 results
data.description:
Citi Research placed Royal Caribbean (RCL) on a downside 30-day short-term view ahead of the company’s fourth quarter results amid expectations that FY26 yield guidance will likely come in below expectations.

Analyst James Hardiman sees enough indications within the cruise industry—including pricing, promotions, and management commentary—that either the demand environment in the sector has decelerated or the supply environment in the crucial Caribbean market has become crowded enough to necessitate a downward revision to Q4 estimates.

“We are again tweaking our estimates to reflect what is likely to be a more conservative set of outlooks when Royal Caribbean (RCL) reports Q4 earnings, with Q1 in particular likely a pain point across the industry given a glut of Caribbean capacity,” Hardiman said in a note to clients.

The capacity issues in the Caribbean have been noted by several Wall Street firms, including Goldman Sachs, which sees net yields in the first half of the year pressured by oversaturation, and Jefferies’ David Katz, who anticipates more pronounced pricing pressures to achieve occupancy targets.

Accordingly, Hardiman lowered his Q4 EPS estimate by just $0.06 to $17.95, coupled with a cut to his yield assumption to 2.65% (vs. 3.0% street estimate) that is largely offset by better cost controls.

Royal Caribbean (RCL) releases  fourth quarter results before the open on January 30, expected to show an unadjusted profit of $2.82 on $4.28B in sales, an increase of 73% and 13%, respectively, from the same quarter last year.  

Royal Caribbean (RCL) shares closed lower again on Wednesday, extending their three-day decline to 10% with another 4% drop.

data.title: San Francisco targets childcare costs in push to retain families
data.description:
San Francisco is set to significantly broaden access to free and subsidized childcare as Mayor Daniel Lurie pushes an affordability agenda aimed at keeping middle-class families in the city.

Under the plan, families earning under $230,000 would receive free childcare for children ages five and younger, while those making up to $310,000 would qualify for partial subsidies. City officials say the move could save families tens of thousands of dollars annually.

The initiative targets one of San Francisco’s steepest living costs: childcare, which can reach $30,000 a year for full-time infant care, according to the Children’s Council of San Francisco. Funding will come from a commercial rent tax approved by voters in 2018, much of whose revenue has remained unused.

Lurie, an heir to the Levi Strauss & Co. fortune, joins a growing group of Democratic leaders prioritizing affordability after the 2024 election cycle. Similar efforts are under way in New York, where Mayor Zohran Mamdani and Gov. Kathy Hochul have pledged expanded early-childhood care.

San Francisco estimates the unused funds will support roughly 20,000 children through 2032, offering what advocates describe as meaningful relief for middle-income households.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
